title: Pipeline as DAG: Cycles, Iteration, and the Loop Region Verdict

current.label: Current State
current.description: What the code actually does today

slides.explanation: Should the mapgen pipeline allow dependency cycles with iteration caps, or iterate whole recipes? **Both — but neither in raw form.** Three layered constructs, each answering a different question. Industry convergence (Blender ships exactly this pair as Repeat Zones vs Simulation Zones) says bounded intra-graph feedback and whole-world evolution are two different problems with two different answers.

layers.title: The three-layer answer
layers.caption: Outer layer is pure discipline, middle layer is the invariant, inner layer is the new construct

layers.label: Recipe as run
layers.description: Pure outer layer: epochs (feed the world back in), parameter search, future evolutionary loop. Zero engine changes — only purity discipline.

layers.label: Acyclic stage DAG
layers.description: The executor schedules stages by artifact dependency. Schedulability, termination, and addressable state are non-negotiable.

layers.label: Loop regions
layers.description: A composite node wrapping an inner acyclic subgraph — the only place feedback lives. Declares carried artifacts, an iteration cap (the contract), and an optional convergence predicate (early exit only).

items.label: Cycles in the executed graph
items.subtitle: ever — loop regions unroll to a DAG

slides.explanation: Ground truth first: today's `PipelineExecutor` is **order-preserving and dependency-validating, not order-computing**. It walks steps in recipe-authored order and throws if a required tag is not yet satisfied. "Turn this into an actual DAG executor" mostly means: make the executor consume the contract that already exists.

items.label: Topological sorts in the executor
items.subtitle: the graph is never computed at runtime

items.label: Schedule source
items.subtitle: the recipe's step sequence IS the schedule

blocks.explanation: The highlighted check is the entire "DAG" behavior at runtime: validate, never schedule.

slides.explanation: The artifact contracts (`requires`/`provides`) are real and typed — but the graph they imply is only consumed by the studio's pipeline view via `buildRecipeDag()`. The executor never consults it. The studio renders a DAG the runtime ignores.

diagram.content:
flowchart LR contracts["Artifact contracts
requires / provides"] --> dag["buildRecipeDag()"] dag --> studio["Studio pipeline view"] contracts --> exec["PipelineExecutor"] exec --> order["Authored order,
validate-only"]
diagram.caption: Two consumers of the same contracts: one draws a graph, the other ignores it

rows: Artifact, Competing writers, Today's behavior
artifact:map.elevation, map-morphology + map-elevation, first writer wins silently
artifact:map.rivers, map-hydrology + map-rivers, first writer wins silently
table.caption: Known mud: duplicate providers — flagged by DAG diagnostics, fatal to any real DAG executor

slides.explanation: The rivers workstream changed ~3 lines of pipeline core — but it produced the best evidence for *why* iteration matters: five couplings the hydrology work wants to express and the current one-pass pipeline cannot. The fifth is not a physics loop at all — it's reactive invalidation, and conflating the two would contaminate both designs.

items.label: Couplings expressible today
items.subtitle: one-pass pipeline; rivers finalize before lakes exist

items.label: Invalidation needs
items.subtitle: belongs in studio re-run machinery, not loops

rows: Coupling, What physics wants, Today
Elevation ↔ discharge ↔ lake level, Re-accumulate flow after depressions fill, One-pass routing; lakes stamped after rivers finalize
Discharge ↔ rainfall budget, Calibrate runoff against precipitation and re-solve, Computed independently; refinement explicitly banned
Rivers ↔ floodplains, Validate outlet coherence after feature placement, No re-validation; conflicts go undetected
SST ↔ ice albedo, Bounded albedo feedback passes, Already spec'd as fixed-iteration — needs executor support
Knob change → selective re-run, Re-run only affected stages in the studio, Full fresh generation on any config change
table.caption: Five pressures from the rivers/hydrology evidence — four physics, one invalidation

slides.explanation: Every mature system that sells graph execution refuses *unstructured* cycles, because they destroy the three properties a graph engine exists to provide: **schedulability** (a topological order exists), **termination** ("is this run done?" is decidable), and **addressable intermediate state** (every value has a name). Every successful re-admission of iteration restores all three by structuring the loop: a delimited region, explicit carried state, and a monotone counter with a cap.

rows: System, Construct, Lesson for us
Houdini, For-each blocks with feedback mode, Loop = delimited region; iteration index is a first-class input
Blender geometry nodes, Repeat Zones vs Simulation Zones, Bounded intra-graph iteration and whole-graph temporal evolution are TWO constructs
Naiad / timely dataflow, Loop counters inside timestamps, Cyclic syntax with acyclic (node × iteration) execution space — the artifact@k trick
Temporal / Argo, Continue-As-New / recursive templates, Recipe-as-run with carried state is production-proven
Datalog / Salsa, Fixed-point only over monotone lattices, Non-monotone physics disqualifies converge-until-stable evaluators
TensorFlow v1, General in-graph cycles, Shipped it; walked it back — the cautionary tale
table.caption: Survey verdicts across workflow engines and procedural tools

slides.explanation: Seven patterns exist in the space, scored for a deterministic, seeded, in-process TypeScript generator with a studio whose killer feature is inspection. Three compose into the recommendation; the dangerous one is T5 — evaluator-level fixed points need monotonicity that erosion physics simply does not have.

radialMetric.title: Patterns adopted
radialMetric.label: T3 + T4 + T6
radialMetric.subtitle: loop regions + iteration-indexed artifacts + outer pass loop
radialMetric.caption: The three adopted patterns compose into one architecture; the other four are rejected, deferred, or absorbed

rows: Pattern, Fit, Why
T1 — unroll at compile time, Strong (v1), Every iteration is an addressable node; zero executor changes
T2 — recursion above the graph, Weak, Recipes are declarative data; imperative control flow does not fit
T3 — delimited loop region, CHOSEN, Houdini-class per-iteration inspection; bounded engine cost
T4 — iteration-indexed artifacts, Adopted as technique, artifact@k keeps the executed graph formally acyclic
T5 — evaluator fixed-point, REJECTED, Physics is not monotone; no termination guarantee available
T6 — outer loop over pass list, Kept as outer layer, Matches simulation practice (operator splitting); = recipe-as-run
T7 — generate-evaluate-mutate, Deferred, Right for parameter search later; wrong grain for state feedback
table.caption: T3 + T4 + T6 compose; T5 is the trap

slides.explanation: The chosen construct. The cycle exists only in the authoring view — at runtime the region **unrolls** into ordinary DAG nodes, so scheduling, caching, and provenance need zero special cases. The studio gets an iteration scrubber for free: every iteration's artifacts exist, addressable, in the artifact store.

diagram.content:
flowchart LR
  climate["climate baseline"] --> erode
  subgraph loop ["loop region: hydrology relax — cap 8, carries elevation@k"]
    erode["erode"] --> fill["fill lakes"]
    fill --> route["re-route rivers"]
    route -. "elevation@k+1" .-> erode
  end
  route --> eco["ecology"]
diagram.caption: Authoring view shows a feedback edge; execution view is an unrolled acyclic chain

rows: Declaration, Meaning, Rule
carried artifacts, What crosses iterations (elevation; discharge), Explicit list — everything else re-reads outer scope
iteration cap, Maximum passes, The contract: determinism by construction
convergence predicate, Pure function of seeded state, Optional; may exit EARLY — never run longer
artifact@k, Iteration-stamped artifact identity, Keeps the executed graph formally acyclic
seed derivation, hash(seed; stepPath; k), Same seed → same map; per-iteration reproducibility
table.caption: The loop region contract — five declarations, no hidden state

blocks.explanation: Fixed counts are what procedural systems near-universally choose (erosion is "N passes", not "until stable") because data-dependent exit plus float drift is the classic same-seed-different-map bug.

html.content:
Authoring view — what you write
loop region: hydrology relax — cap 3
erode→ fill lakes→ re-route rivers
↻ feedback edge carries elevation@k, discharge@k
Execution view — what actually runs (a plain DAG)
iteration cap 3
seed@k = hash(seed, stepPath, k) — same seed, same map, every iteration addressable
html.title: Interactive: drag the cap — the loop unrolls into a DAG
html.caption: Live html block: the authored feedback edge becomes an acyclic chain of iteration-stamped artifacts

slides.explanation: The whole recipe stays a pure function. That single discipline buys epochs ("the world a thousand years later" — feed the world back in as `priorWorld`) and the future evolutionary layer, with zero engine changes. The honest insight from the leverage analysis: **cycles provide none of variation, selection, or retention.**

diagram.content:
flowchart LR inputs["seed + params
+ prior world"] --> recipe["recipe run
(pure function)"] recipe --> world["world"] world --> metrics["quality metrics
(the missing piece)"] metrics -. "select / mutate params" .-> inputs
diagram.caption: The evolutionary loop never looks inside the DAG

objectives.title: Make generation evolutionary
objectives.description: Evolution requires variation, selection, and retention — graph topology provides none of them. The missing piece is the selection signal.

keyResults.text: Map-quality metrics the outer loop can select on
Per-iteration observability in the studio (highest-leverage, cheapest intervention)
Nothing from the engine — the outer loop is a pure layer over the recipe function

slides.explanation: The naming question dissolves once you see the conflation: the current `GenerationPhase` enum values *are* domain names. These are two orthogonal facets. Author the **domain** tag (ownership, vocabulary, who decides). Don't author phase at all — **derive it from DAG rank**, exactly how the studio already positions nodes. The waterfall-progress feeling survives, computed instead of asserted, and it can never drift out of sync with the graph.

blocks.explanation: The highlighted values are domain names wearing a phase costume — `morphology`, `hydrology`, `ecology` are bounded contexts with owners and vocabularies, not positions in a sequence.

rows: Facet, What it means, Where it comes from
Domain, Ownership + vocabulary: morphology owns its ops and strategies, Authored tag on the stage
Phase, Position in the waterfall — progress sense for UI and scheduling lanes, Derived from dependency rank — never authored
Gate, A tag earns existence only if a consumer uses it, Scheduler behavior / validation / studio query — else drop it
table.caption: Two facets, two sources — never one enum

slides.explanation: Three semantic decisions, all following the same rule: fix the concepts first, choose the encoding second. A step's identity is its place in an authored sequence; a procedure's identity is its callable contract — equating them is a modeling claim with no competency question behind it today.

rows: Question, Decision, Reasoning
Steps as oRPC procedures?, Adopt the discipline; not the transport, Contract-first typed I/O schemas: yes. Marshaling the context across a network boundary with no consumer: no
Stage-level authored run()?, Yes, Explicit step sequence — transparent and scriptable. Executor schedules stages; the stage runs its steps
Strategies via oRPC middleware?, No — compile-time pure-function binding, Strategy selection is a compile-time configuration decision visible in the plan; middleware is runtime indirection
table.caption: Concepts before encoding, every time

blocks.explanation: This split is also the pace-layering the system-design mandate requires: a heavily-contracted slow core (the executor), and fast replaceable layers (stage internals, strategy bindings) that can churn without destabilizing it.

slides.explanation: Reversibility-calibrated sequencing: each step is independently shippable, and the loop construct can be deleted back to unrolling at any point. The executor contract is the irreversible piece, so it moves last and smallest.

objectives.title: v0 — clean the graph
objectives.description: Pure cleanup, do now: any DAG-consuming executor chokes on duplicate providers.

keyResults.text: Duplicate artifact providers for map.elevation and map.rivers

objectives.title: v1 — compile-time unrolling
objectives.description: The cheapest honest step: fixed-count loops expand into explicit step instances at recipe compile time. Zero executor changes.

objectives.title: v2 — loop regions as sugar
objectives.description: First-class authoring construct that compiles down to v1 unrolling — fully deletable.

keyResults.text: Loop region declaration: carried artifacts + cap + optional predicate
Runtime exit predicates only when a real recipe demands one

objectives.title: v3 — selection layer (separate track)
objectives.description: Pure layer over the recipe function; never touches the engine.

keyResults.text: Evolutionary outer loop over (seed, params, priorWorld)

slides.explanation: Every frame needs its kill-switch. The probe: author the first real feedback recipe — **depression-fill → re-accumulate discharge → re-project rivers** is the best candidate from the rivers evidence — against the loop-region design *before* freezing the contract.

rows: Risk control, Statement

Falsifier, If the first real feedback recipe requires the executor to special-case any stage — or state must escape the region — the seam is wrong; promote the structural alternative (field-level dataflow with fixpoint semantics)
Escape ramp, Loop regions compile to unrolling — the construct can be deleted without stranding any recipe
Accepted trade-off, We give up generality (no dynamic topology; no unbounded cycles; no distributed execution) for debuggability and deterministic replay
Watch items, Cap-tuning culture replacing operator fixes; per-iteration snapshot memory cost in the studio; authors retreating to linear recipes if authoring gets noisy
table.caption: The recommendation carries its own refutation conditions

explainer.content: **Full investigation record** — workstream frame, four evidence lanes (design-branch architecture map, rivers-lineage deltas, cognition-skill mandates, prior-art survey), the 28-point mandate checklist, deferred items, and the zero-context Next Packet.
